YOUR OFE 2025 REPORT

Author

Farmers DataLab

Published

April 9, 2026

2025 Cropping Season Field Kolb2  |  NRate 74.5 lb N/ac  |  Research question: Will a clover cover crop supply 60 lbs of nitrogen?
⬇ Download PDF

Welcome to your 2025 farm report. This report summarizes the on-farm nitrogen experiment conducted in your Kolb2 field, where clover cover crop strips (treatment) were compared against no-cover-crop controls.

Take-home message: Across all in-season nitrogen indicators (NBI, chlorophyll, biomass %N, and grain %N) corn plants in the clover cover crop strips consistently showed higher nitrogen status than control plots, and this translated into a meaningful yield advantage of +16 bu/ac (+11.8%) at harvest. That said, end-of-season cornstalk nitrate values fell below the 250 ppm sufficiency threshold in both strips, suggesting that while the cover crop improved early-season nitrogen availability, the full 60 lb N/ac target was likely not met, making direct clover biomass sampling in 2026 the key next step.

Results Summary

Metric Control Cover (Treatment) Difference p-value
🌽 Corn Yield 76.9 89.2 +12.3 (16%) p = 0.00118
🌿 NBI (Nitrogen Balance Index) 43 48.4 +5.4 (12.6%) p < 0.001
🍃 Chlorophyll Index 2.392% 2.828% +18.2% n too small*
🧪 Biomass %N — (MaizeBiomass) 1.085% 1.262% +16.3% p = 0.00729
🌾 Grain %N (MaizeGrain) 74 ppm 57 ppm -17 ppm n = 1
🌽 CSNT (Cornstalk Nitrate) 136.7 bu/ac 152.8 bu/ac +16.1 bu/ac (+11.8%) spatial data†

* Biomass C:N sample sizes are small (n = 5 Control, n = 5 Treatment). Effect sizes (Cohen’s d) are reported instead of p-values for that section. † Yield monitor data contains ~130,000 GPS points with spatial autocorrelation — a simple p-value is not appropriate. The difference is reported as a descriptive field-level observation.

🌿 Nitrogen Balance Index (NBI)

The Nitrogen Balance Index (NBI) is measured with the Dualex sensor and reflects the ratio of chlorophyll to flavonoids in the leaf. A higher NBI indicates better nitrogen status: the plant has enough N to build chlorophyll without accumulating excess flavonoids as a stress response. Two leaves per plant were measured and averaged. Results are compared between Control (no cover crop) and Treatment (clover cover crop) strips using a two-sample t-test.

Cover (Treatment)
89.2 +12.3 vs control (16%)
Control mean: 76.9
Effect Size
16%
relative difference treatment vs control
p = 0.00118 ✓
  • Density
  • Boxplot

🍃 Chlorophyll (Chl)

The Chlorophyll Index (Chl) is also measured with the Dualex sensor and estimates the amount of chlorophyll in the leaf. Chlorophyll is directly linked to the plant’s capacity for photosynthesis and is a reliable proxy for nitrogen sufficiency. Higher values indicate greener, more nitrogen-sufficient leaves. As with NBI, two leaves per plant were averaged and treatments compared with a t-test.

Cover (Treatment)
48.4 +5.4 vs control (12.6%)
Control mean: 43
Effect Size
12.6%
relative difference treatment vs control
p < 0.001 ✓
  • Density
  • Boxplot

🧪 C:N in Corn Biomass —

Corn biomass was collected and sent for laboratory C:N analysis. This measures the actual nitrogen concentration in the plant tissue, a direct indicator of how much N the plant was able to take up during the critical early growth window. Note: sample sizes are small (n = 5 Control, n = 5 Treatment), so results are descriptive. Effect sizes (Cohen’s d) are reported instead of p-values.

Total Nitrogen in Biomass (%N)

Cover (Treatment) — Mean %N (Biomass)
2.828 +18.2% vs Control
Control mean: 2.392
Cohen's d (effect size)
-1.53
n = 5 Control · n = 5 Treatment

16.54 -15.6% vs Control
Control mean: 19.59

1.36
Lower C:N = more nitrogen-rich tissue

🌾 C:N in Corn Grain

Grain samples were collected and analyzed for C:N content. Nitrogen concentration in grain reflects the plant’s ability to remobilize N during grain fill, a final-season indicator of whether the cover crop influenced overall nitrogen availability through the growing season.

Total Nitrogen in Grain (%N)

Cover (Treatment)
1.262 +0.177 vs control (16.3%)
Control mean: 1.085
Effect Size
16.3%
relative difference treatment vs control
p = 0.00729 ✓

🌽 Cornstalk Nitrate Test (CSNT)

The Cornstalk Nitrate Test (CSNT) measures nitrate concentration in the lower cornstalk at the end of the season. It is a post-harvest diagnostic tool: values above 250 ppm suggest the crop had sufficient nitrogen; values below 250 ppm suggest nitrogen was limiting. For Kolb2 field, both the control and cover treatment fell below 250 ppm, indicating that nitrogen was limiting in both strips. Notably, the cover treatment measured lower than the control (57 vs 74 ppm), though both are in the same deficient range. Because only one sample per treatment was collected, no statistical comparison is possible — this is a field-level observation.

Control — CSNT
74 ppm
⚠ Below 250 ppm threshold — N was limiting
Cover (Treatment) — CSNT
57 ppm
⚠ Below 250 ppm threshold — N was limiting  |  n = 1 per treatment

🌱 Soil Health Assessment

Soil health was assessed using the Cornell Soil Health Test. The overall quality score was 78/100 (High). Key items to watch: Soil pH scored 28 (Low, orange) at 5.7, below the optimal 6.2 - 6.8 range for corn, which can reduce nutrient availability and should be addressed with lime. Soil Respiration (score 56) and Active Carbon (score 54) are both in the Medium range, indicating microbial activity and labile carbon could improve. Physical and most biological indicators are strong: Aggregate Stability (100), Organic Matter (100), and AWC (93) reflect the benefits of no-till management. Aluminum (105.1 ppm) is flagged as excessive, which is linked to the low pH.

📝 Conclusion

🌿 Leaf Nitrogen Status (NBI)
Cover crop plots showed a 16% difference in NBI compared to control (89.2 vs 76.9, p = 0.00118). Chlorophyll also differed by 12.6% (p < 0.001). These in-season readings capture the nitrogen status.
🧪 Biomass Nitrogen (%N)
Laboratory analysis of corn biomass showed cover plots had +18.2% difference in tissue nitrogen (2.828% vs 2.392%). Cohen's d = -1.53. Small sample size (n = 5 vs 5) — descriptive only.
🌾 Grain Nitrogen (%N)
At harvest, grain %N was +16.3% different in cover strips (1.262% vs 1.085%, p = 0.00729, n = 10 vs 20). Grain N reflects the full-season nitrogen story, integrating both applied fertilizer and any cover crop contribution.
🌽 CSNT — N Limiting in Both Strips
End-of-season CSNT values were below 250 ppm in both treatments (Control: 74 ppm; Cover: 57 ppm). This indicates the corn crop was nitrogen-limited regardless of the cover crop treatment. The 60 lb/ac clover N target was not demonstrably met this season — direct clover biomass sampling is needed to quantify actual N supply in 2026.
🌽 Corn Yield — Cover vs No Cover
Yield monitor data showed cover crop strips averaged 152.8 bu/ac vs 136.7 bu/ac in control strips — a difference of +16.1 bu/ac (+11.8%). Given the spatial autocorrelation inherent in yield monitor data, this is a descriptive field observation rather than a formal statistical result, but the direction is consistent with the in-season nitrogen indicators.
